summary: Jeff Atwood argues that meaningful success in technology and creative endeavors requires years of consistent effort, not overnight breakthroughs. Using examples from Gmail's seven-year journey to success, the Beatles' decade of practice before Sgt. Peppers, and his own blog's three years to gain readership, he demonstrates that the 'overnight success' narrative is both misleading and harmful. He advocates for finding intrinsic satisfaction in the process itself rather than fixating on outcomes, noting that Stack Overflow was built with the same long-term mindset. The key is deliberate practice combined with frugality to sustain the long journey.
key_points: Gmail took 7.5 years from inception to mainstream success, facing internal skepticism and predictions of doom even at launch
Peter Norvig's research shows expertise in any field requires about ten years of deliberate practice—there are no shortcuts, even for prodigies like Mozart
The 'overnight success' myth is harmful because it creates unrealistic expectations and causes people to give up prematurely
The process of building something meaningful should be inherently satisfying—success becomes almost irrelevant when you love the daily work
Atwood's own blog required three years of posting 3-5 times per week before achieving any real popularity in the software development community
money_quotes: This notion of overnight success is very misleading, and rather harmful. If you're starting something new, expect a long journey.
You must move very fast, otherwise you will never arrive, because it's a long journey!
The process is inherently satisfying. Waking up every day and doing something you love – even better, surrounded by a community who loves it too – is its own reward.
There appear to be no real shortcuts: even Mozart, who was a musical prodigy at age 4, took 13 more years before he began to produce world-class music.
I fully expect to be writing on this blog, in one form or another, for the rest of my life. It is a part of who I am.
Whether you ultimately achieve readers, or pageviews, or whatever high score table it is we're measuring this week, try to remember it's worth doing because, well – it's worth doing.
If you keep doing it long enough, who knows? You might very well wake up one day and find out you're an overnight success.
key_insight: True success requires years of deliberate, consistent effort, but the journey becomes sustainable when you find the process itself inherently rewarding rather than fixating on outcomes.
